duration: 5 obsevations, all less than one minute each
specific_location: 66 Casa Del Monte Ct No Name
description: Here is my brief write-up on the BLUH that was posted on the western slope birding network list on July 1, 2010. The bird did not stick around and was only seen on 6/30 by myself and my wife Kay, too bad as the bird deserved to be shared with others. This was certainly a species I never expected to see at my home, there aren't many records in Colorado and this was a state bird for me.   > Late yesterday afternoon (6/30/11) and through the early evening we were visited  > multiple times by a large dark female hummingbird. The bird was about  > size of a Pine Siskin (in fact, I wondered at first glimpse what is a Pine Siskin doing around the hummingbird feeders), nothing remarkable about the bill, black  > through the eye, and white markings on the face (I didn't note these  > well enough to really describe them). Breast and belly were uniformly  > gray, not speckled. The underside of the tail is what I really  > focused on. It was dark, not green and not at all forked, with a huge  > amount of white at the tips of the outer feathers. In most poses the  > bird's tail feathers were not flared, and the tail would appear to  > have a 3/8" wide white tail-band . I studied the bird during it's first few visits and then attempted a photo with no success. > Breast coloring, and tail shape and color are not consistent with  > Magnificent Hummingbird even though that would be the most likely big  > hummer around here. I have seen a couple of female Magnificent  > Hummers in Colorado and many in SE Arizona. My first impression, "This  > is not a Magnificent Hummingbird"! >  > The good news, this bird was most certainly a Blue-throated Hummingbird. > BOWC describes seven late summer records in western Colorado, none as  > early as this one. Bob & Bob mention 2 late June records in Grand  > County and El Paso County. The bad news, we have not seen the bird  > after around 8PM yesterday evening in spite of an intensive feeder  > watch by Larry Arnold this morning. Should the bird return, we will  > figure out a way that folks come and visit. I have my fingers crossed. >  > Tom McConnell > Glenwood Springs >Behaviors: Feeding at sugar-water feederCall: Did Not vocalizePlumage: Adult basic
